title: Orchard Establishment & Food Forest Design
pillar_name: Regenerative Food Systems, Animal Stewardship & Land Care

competence_markers.description: The student can match tree species and varieties to site conditions (hardiness, soil, sun, spacing)
The student has established fruit trees or berry bushes and managed them through at least one season
The student can prune a fruit tree and explain every cut: heading versus thinning, structural goals, and expected results
The student can describe the seven-layer food forest model and design a guild for a specific tree
The student has practiced grafting with at least one successful take
The student maintains records for perennial plantings including growth, management, and pest observations

content.why_this_matters:
You have planted a tree and watched it grow. Now you learn to establish and manage orchards and food forests — perennial systems that produce food for decades while building soil, creating habitat, and becoming more productive over time. This is the longest-horizon thinking in agriculture, and it is among the most rewarding.

An orchard is not a garden. You do not replant it every year. The decisions you make at establishment — site selection, variety choice, spacing, rootstock — are decisions you live with for decades. Getting them right is the difference between thirty years of abundant fruit and thirty years of frustration. This packet teaches you to make those decisions well and to manage the trees through the critical early years.

A food forest takes the orchard concept further: stacking multiple layers of food-producing plants — trees, shrubs, herbs, ground cover, vines — into a designed ecosystem that mimics natural forest succession. The food forest does not require the annual replanting that a garden demands. Once established, it produces with decreasing management input and increasing ecological value.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Site selection and variety matching** — choosing the right plant for the right place
2. **Fruit tree establishment** — planting, staking, watering, and managing trees through their critical first years
3. **Pruning as a practiced skill** — heading cuts, thinning cuts, central leader, open center, scaffold selection
4. **Food forest design** — the seven-layer model, guild planting, succession ecology
5. **Grafting** — propagating specific varieties onto appropriate rootstocks
6. **Integration with other systems** — connecting perennials to the garden, animals, and water management
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Site Selection and Variety Matching

The single most important decision in perennial food production is matching the plant to the site. A fruit tree planted in the wrong place will underperform for its entire life. You cannot move it once it is established.

**Cold hardiness zones:** Every perennial plant has a hardiness range — the coldest temperatures it can survive. Your USDA hardiness zone tells you the average annual minimum temperature. Plant only varieties rated for your zone or colder. A zone 6 plant in a zone 4 climate will die.

**Microclimates:** Within any property, microclimates create warmer and colder spots. South-facing slopes warm earlier in spring. Low spots collect cold air (cold air is dense and flows downhill like water). A fruit tree planted on a south-facing slope may bloom two weeks earlier than one in a valley — which is an advantage if it avoids late frost and a disaster if it does not. Learn your property's microclimates before planting.

**Soil requirements:** Most fruit trees need well-drained soil. Waterlogged roots kill faster than drought. Some species tolerate heavy soil (pears, plums); others demand excellent drainage (peaches, cherries). Match the species to your soil, or amend the site.

**Sun exposure:** Fruit trees need full sun — minimum 6–8 hours of direct sunlight daily. Partial shade reduces flowering, fruit quality, and disease resistance.

**Spacing:** Plant for the mature canopy size, not the size of the tree at planting. A standard apple tree needs 25–30 feet between trees. A dwarf apple needs 8–10 feet. Crowded trees shade each other, compete for resources, and are harder to manage.

### Part 2: Fruit Tree Establishment

The first 2–3 years after planting determine the tree's long-term health and productivity.

**Planting depth:** The graft union must stay above the soil line. If buried, the scion variety will root on its own and you lose the dwarfing or disease-resistance benefits of the rootstock.

**Watering:** New trees need consistent moisture — deep watering once or twice a week during the first growing season. Not a daily sprinkle; a deep soaking that reaches the entire root zone.

**Mulching:** Spread mulch 3–4 inches deep in a 3–4 foot diameter around the tree. Keep mulch 6 inches from the trunk. Mulch piled against the trunk (volcano mulching) is one of the most common and most damaging errors in tree care — it traps moisture against the bark, invites rot and rodent damage, and can kill the tree.

**Staking:** Most new trees benefit from staking for the first year to prevent wind damage. Use a flexible tie that allows some trunk movement — a rigidly staked tree does not develop trunk strength. Remove the stake after one year.

**Initial pruning:** At planting, make formative pruning cuts to establish the tree's structure. For a central-leader tree: select 3–4 well-spaced scaffold branches with wide angles (45–60 degrees from the trunk) and remove competing branches. For an open-center tree: head the central leader to encourage lateral growth and select scaffold branches that form a vase shape.

### Part 3: Pruning — The Central Orchard Skill

Pruning is the skill that separates productive orchards from overgrown, unproductive ones. Every cut should have a purpose you can explain.

**Two types of cuts:**
- **Heading cuts** (cutting a branch partway back): Stimulates branching below the cut. Use to encourage a branch to fill a gap or to manage tree height. Heading cuts make the tree bushier.
- **Thinning cuts** (removing a branch entirely at its base): Opens the canopy, directs energy to remaining branches, and lets light and air in. Thinning cuts make the tree more open and productive.

**Training systems:**
- **Central leader:** One dominant vertical trunk with scaffold branches arranged around it. Strong, natural shape. Best for apples on vigorous rootstock, pears, and most nut trees.
- **Open center (vase):** No central leader. Three to four main scaffold branches form a vase shape, letting light into the interior. Best for peaches, plums, and cherries.

**When to prune:** Most pruning is done in late winter while the tree is dormant. Summer pruning is used to control vigor — removing new growth to reduce tree size and redirect energy to fruit production.

**The rule:** Never remove more than one-third of a tree's canopy in a single year unless you have specific justification. Over-pruning shocks the tree and triggers excessive regrowth (water sprouts).

### Part 4: Food Forest Design — The Seven Layers

A food forest stacks multiple layers of productive plants into a designed ecosystem:

1. **Canopy:** Large fruit and nut trees (apple, pear, walnut, chestnut)
2. **Sub-canopy:** Smaller trees (dwarf apple, peach, plum, cherry, mulberry)
3. **Shrub layer:** Berry bushes (blueberry, currant, gooseberry, elderberry, hazelnut)
4. **Herbaceous layer:** Perennial herbs and vegetables (comfrey, rhubarb, artichoke, medicinal herbs)
5. **Ground cover:** Low-growing plants (creeping thyme, strawberry, white clover)
6. **Vine layer:** Climbing plants (grape, kiwi, hardy passionfruit)
7. **Root layer:** Root crops (burdock, skirret, horseradish)

The design mimics natural forest succession — a complex, multi-layered ecosystem that is more productive, more resilient, and more self-maintaining than any single-layer planting. Each layer fills a different light and space niche. The whole produces more food per acre than any layer alone.

### Part 5: Guild Planting

A guild is a designed community of plants around a central tree, where each plant serves a specific function:

- **Nitrogen fixers:** White clover, vetch, lupine — feed nitrogen to the tree through root nodule bacteria
- **Dynamic accumulators:** Comfrey, yarrow — deep roots mine minerals from subsoil; when cut, the leaves release those minerals on the surface as mulch
- **Pest confusers:** Aromatic herbs — confuse pest insects with strong scents
- **Beneficial insect attractors:** Flowers — feed parasitic wasps, ladybugs, and other predators
- **Ground covers:** Suppress weeds, retain moisture, protect soil

A well-designed guild reduces management input (fewer weeds, fewer pests, self-generating fertility) while increasing the tree's health and productivity.

### Part 6: Grafting

Grafting is the technique of joining a piece of one variety (the scion) onto the root system of another (the rootstock). Every named apple variety — Honeycrisp, Fuji, Granny Smith — is genetically identical to every other tree of that variety, propagated by grafting for decades or centuries.

**Whip-and-tongue graft:** The most common method for young trees. Both scion and rootstock are cut at matching angles with interlocking tongues, bound together with tape, and sealed with wax or Parafilm. When successful, the two pieces grow together as one tree.

**Why graft?** You cannot grow a Honeycrisp apple from a Honeycrisp seed — the seed produces a random cross that may or may not taste good. Grafting is how you reproduce a specific, desirable variety. It also allows you to control tree size (through rootstock selection) and adapt a variety to soils it could not grow in on its own roots.

Practice grafting on inexpensive rootstock. Expect your first attempts to fail. By the fifth or sixth attempt, you should be getting consistent takes.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Site Selection and Variety Matching
**Developing well:** The student evaluates a planting site systematically — checking hardiness zone, noting microclimates, assessing soil drainage, measuring sun exposure, and calculating mature spacing. They reject inappropriate species-site combinations without prompting. They consult extension resources for regional variety recommendations.

**Developing slowly:** The student selects varieties based on what they want to eat rather than what the site can support. They ignore spacing requirements or assume they can compensate for poor site conditions later. They do not check hardiness zones.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the student actually walked the planting site at different times of day? Do they understand that a south-facing slope and a north-facing slope on the same property can be dramatically different environments? Have they read their local extension fruit-growing guide? If not, these are the gaps to fill before planting.

### 2. Tree Establishment
**Developing well:** The student plants trees at correct depth (graft union visible above soil), mulches properly (away from trunk, 3-4 inches deep in a wide ring), waters deeply on a schedule, and checks on new plantings regularly. They can explain why volcano mulching is harmful.

**Developing slowly:** The student plants trees and then neglects follow-up care. Mulch is piled against trunks. Watering is sporadic. The student treats planting day as the finish line rather than the starting line.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the student checking new plantings at least weekly during the first growing season? Do they understand that most tree deaths in the first two years come from neglect after planting, not from planting errors? Assign a specific check schedule and hold them to it.

### 3. Pruning Competence
**Developing well:** The student approaches a tree, assesses its structure, and makes deliberate cuts they can explain. They distinguish heading from thinning cuts and use each appropriately. They prune toward a specific training system (central leader or open center) matched to the species. They stop when they have removed enough rather than continuing to "clean up."

**Developing slowly:** The student cuts randomly, cannot explain their decisions, or is paralyzed by fear of making a mistake. They either shear the tree like a hedge or refuse to remove enough wood to make a difference.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the student practiced on real trees — not just studied pruning in a book? Fear of cutting is normal and usually resolves with guided practice. Start with obvious removals: dead wood, crossing branches, suckers. Build confidence with clear-cut decisions before moving to judgment calls about structure.

### 4. Food Forest Design
**Developing well:** The student designs for a real site with species selected for each of the seven layers, appropriate to climate and site conditions. Guild designs include nitrogen fixers, dynamic accumulators, ground covers, and pest-management plants with specific species named. The design includes a realistic establishment sequence and management plan.

**Developing slowly:** The student copies a generic food forest template without adapting it to their site. Species are selected from books without checking hardiness or site suitability. The design has no management plan or establishment sequence.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the student actually walked the design site? Can they explain why they chose each species for each position? If the design is generic, require them to defend every species choice against their site's specific conditions. Remove anything they cannot justify.

### 5. Grafting
**Developing well:** The student has attempted multiple grafts with increasingly clean technique. At least one graft has taken. They can explain the purpose of grafting and the relationship between scion and rootstock.

**Developing slowly:** The student has not attempted grafting, or has attempted once and given up after failure.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the student had enough practice material? Grafting requires repetition. Provide more rootstock and scion wood. Demonstrate the technique again. If knife handling is the issue, practice the cuts on willow or other soft wood before using valuable scion material.
